current_fact.fact_text: In 1889, a cannon shot at noon unleashed the Land Rush of 1889, sending thousands of settlers racing across the Oklahoma plains to hammer stakes into ‘unassigned’ lands. Entire cities like Oklahoma City and Guthrie went from empty prairie to bustling towns of 10,000 people in mere hours.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

current_fact.match_reason: The frantic overland rush for new land mirrors, in a calmer key, the modern urge to set out into open spaces; a sailing‑trip episode captures that same frontier instinct of moving toward a horizon and making temporary homes together.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1898, as tensions with Spain boiled over, U.S. President William McKinley called for 125,000 volunteers and expanded the regular Army to 65,000, gearing the country for the Spanish–American War. A short, sharp conflict over Cuba and beyond would end with the U.S. unexpectedly holding far‑flung island territories.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A war that reshaped empires pairs with an episode about a band whose entire existence critiques militarism, imperialism, and power — turning historical calls to arms into calls to question.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1915, clouds of chlorine gas rolled over trenches at the Second Battle of Ypres, marking the first large‑scale use of poison gas in World War I. The greenish mist turned the air itself into a weapon, permanently changing how humanity thought about chemistry, ethics, and war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra
songs: The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind

queue.match_reason: The misuse of chemistry to weaponize the air contrasts with an episode about choosing what we let into our minds; one shows how technology can harm, the other how attention can heal and re‑humanize our use of tools.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1944, as part of Operation Persecution, Allied forces landed near Hollandia in New Guinea, using surprise and overwhelming airpower to outmaneuver entrenched Japanese positions. The amphibious assault effectively leapfrogged enemy strongholds by using geography and logistics as strategic instruments.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A sudden, high‑impact amphibious landing feels like an electric jolt in military history; a blistering rock episode mirrors that surge of energy, precision, and risk.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1944, on the icy fringes of Greenland, a tiny Allied Sledge Patrol attacked and destroyed the German Bassgeiger weather station. Though remote, this clash in the ‘weather war’ was crucial, because accurate Arctic forecasts could decide the fate of convoys and battles thousands of kilometers away.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: An obscure Arctic skirmish over weather data pairs with an episode immersed in polar seas; both reveal how remote, frozen places quietly shape the fate and stories of people far away.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1944, Sikorsky R‑4 helicopters flew into the China–Burma–India Theater to perform the first combat search‑and‑rescue missions in history. These fragile, buzzing machines opened a new chapter in aviation, proving that vertical flight could pull people out of places no plane could reach.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: Helicopter rescue is literally about entering danger to pull someone back to life; an episode about immersion and resurfacing mirrors that movement between being lost and being lifted out.

queue.fact_text: On this date in 1930, the United Kingdom, Japan, and the United States signed the London Naval Treaty, capping ship numbers and even trying to regulate submarine warfare. It was an ambitious attempt to place rules and limits on the world’s most powerful weapons as they floated silently beneath the waves.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A treaty trying to impose rules on a deep, fluid battlefield meets an episode that explicitly suspends rules in favor of drifting; one is about controlling flows of steel, the other about surrendering to flows of sound.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1951, Chinese forces launched fierce assaults against Australian and Canadian positions at the Battle of Kapyong during the Korean War. In a narrow valley, small UN units held against overwhelming numbers, buying vital time for Seoul’s defenses.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A desperate stand in a Korean valley contrasts with an episode about moving through landscapes in a reflective way; where soldiers once held ground under fire, the music now invites a gentler, more conscious passage through the world.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1954, millions of Americans watched the Army–McCarthy hearings unfold live on television, seeing a senator’s fear‑driven accusations challenged in real time. It was one of the first moments when broadcast media helped turn the tide against political paranoia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: The public watched McCarthyism unravel and collectively ‘think again’ about fear and accusation; an episode about unlearning and revisiting beliefs amplifies that moment of televised re‑evaluation.

queue.fact_text: In 1969, British yachtsman Sir Robin Knox‑Johnston completed the first solo non‑stop circumnavigation of the globe, winning the Sunday Times Golden Globe Race. For 312 days he sailed alone through every mood of the ocean, returning not with a trophy in hand, but with an unbroken circle on the map.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

queue.match_reason: Months alone at sea are full of literal emptiness around the boat; an episode reframing emptiness as creative space echoes how Knox‑Johnston turned isolation into a pioneering voyage.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1970, millions of people took to streets, campuses, and parks to celebrate the first Earth Day, staging teach‑ins and demonstrations for a planet in trouble. The event helped galvanize modern environmentalism and led to concrete protections like the U.S. Clean Air Act and the Environmental Protection Agency.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A global wake‑up call about caring for our home pairs with a gently named ‘Slow Planet’ episode; both invite us to decelerate, pay attention to Earth, and choose softer rhythms.

queue.fact_text: Also on April 22, 1970, Chicano residents of San Diego occupied space beneath the Coronado Bridge, refusing to see it turned into a highway patrol station and instead claiming it as Chicano Park. Over time, they transformed the gray concrete pylons into a forest of vivid murals, turning protest into a permanent open‑air gallery.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Chicano Park’s murals and activism are deeply tied to Mexican‑American and Spanish‑language culture; an episode on Flamenco’s history resonates with the shared Iberian and Latin heritage pulsing under that bridge.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 1977, a telephone call first traveled not over copper wires but through strands of glass, when optical fiber carried live telephone traffic for the very first time. Pulses of light began to replace electrical signals, quietly laying the groundwork for the global internet.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: The first light‑based phone call set the stage for always‑online lives; an episode about our tangled relationship with phones and the internet is a natural companion to the birth of fiber‑optic communication.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 2005, Japanese Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi offered a formal apology for Japan’s wartime actions, acknowledging the ‘tremendous damage and suffering’ Japan had caused in Asia. It was a politically fraught, emotionally charged attempt to speak across generations about memory, guilt, and reconciliation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A national apology is a kind of political storm breaking after decades of clouds; a reflective, hopeful episode about weathering change and trusting that it can’t rain forever underscores that fragile turn toward healing.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 2016, the Paris Agreement was formally signed at the United Nations, with countries pledging to limit global warming well below 2°C. It was an unprecedented planetary contract in which almost every nation agreed to try to reshape its energy systems for the sake of a shared climate future.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The Paris Agreement asks the world to learn, adapt, and keep trying despite setbacks; an episode about the slow joy of learning and accepting failure mirrors the kind of long‑term persistence climate action demands.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 2025, tourists traveling through the scenic town of Pahalgam in Jammu and Kashmir were attacked by militants, leaving at least 26 people dead. The assault, claimed by The Resistance Front, tore violence into a place better known for its mountains, rivers, and pilgrim trails.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A brutal attack in a place of beauty asks for thoughtful, gentle reflection rather than spectacle; an episode about healing from sudden trauma and learning to move again offers that kind of contemplative space.

queue.fact_text: World War II also touched Greenland on April 22, 1944, when a small Danish–Norwegian ‘Sledge Patrol’ on dog sleds confronted and attacked the German Bassgeiger weather station in the Arctic wilderness. It was a tiny raid in a vast white landscape, fought more with endurance and navigation than with bullets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: Both the Arctic Sledge Patrol and Muay Thai training rely on stamina, discipline, and using the body in extreme conditions; a physically intense Thai boxing episode reflects the quiet toughness of those sled‑borne fighters.

queue.fact_text: Back in 1944’s Pacific War, Operation Persecution’s landings around Hollandia showed how airpower, logistics, and code‑breaking could make heavily fortified bases suddenly obsolete. Japanese troops there found themselves cut off by a strategy that leapfrogged geography as if it were a board game.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: Operation Persecution required Allied commanders to change roles and tactics rapidly, almost like adopting new ‘alter egos’ in strategy; an episode about shapeshifting identities parallels that flexible, reality‑bending approach to warfare.

queue.fact_text: On April 22, 2005, as he apologized for wartime aggression, Prime Minister Koizumi also sparked heated debate at home and abroad over how history should be remembered. The speech highlighted how collective memory is less like a fixed archive and more like a song that each generation remixes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A controversial national apology and its contested memory parallel Residente’s own work of revisiting history, confronting injustice, and turning political and personal reckoning into art.

queue.fact_text: In 1977’s first fiber‑optic phone call, voices were translated into pulses of light racing through hair‑thin glass. Half a century later, those same principles carry our music streams, video calls, and late‑night Sedna sessions across oceans at nearly the speed of light.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: Light‑speed communication and futuristic electronic dance share the same mood of glowing, high‑tech motion; a ‘cosmic’ Italo‑disco journey is a fitting soundtrack for humanity learning to speak in photons.

published.fact_text: In 1500, Portuguese navigator Pedro Álvares Cabral’s fleet, aiming for India, was blown far into the Atlantic and instead made landfall on the coast of what is now Brazil. That ‘navigational detour’ accidentally folded an entire new continent into Portugal’s maritime empire and began centuries of cultural and musical exchange across the Atlantic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: Cabral’s landing opened the Atlantic world where African, European, and Indigenous cultures mixed; an Afrobeat history set perfectly underscores that long echo of colonial routes reshaping global music.

published.fact_text: On April 22, 1529, the Treaty of Zaragoza drew an invisible line across the eastern hemisphere, slicing the globe between Spanish and Portuguese spheres of influence east of the Maluku Islands. It was a cartographic power move that treated entire oceans and cultures as negotiable coordinates.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Creûza de mä

published.match_reason: A treaty about carving up seas and trade routes pairs with an episode that sails musically across historically contested waters, reclaiming them as shared cultural space instead of colonial borders.

published.fact_text: In 1519, Hernán Cortés founded a precarious coastal settlement at Veracruz, Mexico, effectively burning his bridges to Spain and anchoring the Spanish conquest on the American mainland. That small outpost became a gateway through which ships, silver, people, and music would flow for centuries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: Veracruz’s port helped seed the Afro‑Latin cultural circuits that much later gave birth to genres like reggaeton; an episode unpacking that music’s roots echoes the deep colonial currents behind it.

published.fact_text: On April 22, 1809, Napoleon’s forces defeated the Austrian army at the Battle of Eckmühl and drove them across the Danube at Regensburg. The clash was a chess move in a larger European struggle, fought in springtime fields that would soon bloom where artillery had just scorched the earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A brutal 19th‑century battlefield contrasts with the liberating defiance of women in rock; guitars as a new kind of weapon replacing muskets and cannons with distortion and dissent.

published.fact_text: In 1889, a cannon shot at noon unleashed the Land Rush of 1889, sending thousands of settlers racing across the Oklahoma plains to hammer stakes into ‘unassigned’ lands. Entire cities like Oklahoma City and Guthrie went from empty prairie to bustling towns of 10,000 people in mere hours.

episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: The frantic overland rush for new land mirrors, in a calmer key, the modern urge to set out into open spaces; a sailing‑trip episode captures that same frontier instinct of moving toward a horizon and making temporary homes together.
